summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – INJUNCTIONS – Where the mother seeks injunctions restraining the father from communicating to the public an account of these proceedings – Where the father has made a number of social media posts which detail aspects of these proceedings – Where the injunctions sought will supplement compliance with s 114Q of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) as there are special circumstances present – Injunctions granted restraining the father from communicating to the public an account of these proceedings and requiring him to remove previously posted communications.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

This matter came before me on 12 March 2026 for interim hearing, following the mother’s filing of an Application in a Proceeding (first filed on 13 February 2026 and subsequently amended on 10 March 2026), in which, as amended, she sought orders for:
(a) The referral of this matter to the Australian Federal Police for investigation into whether the father has breached s 114Q and s 114R of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”);

(b) Injunctions against the father, restraining him from filming/voice recording the mother at changeover or at any other time and from discussing the family law proceedings with anyone other than his legal representatives or publishing any information pertaining to the family law proceedings;

(c) The father to undertake a psychiatric assessment; and

(d) Costs.

BACKGROUND

The current substantive proceedings between the parents regard the care arrangements for their child, X (“the child”), born in 2018 and who is seven years of age. Those issues have been listed for final hearing for five days commencing on 29 June 2026.
The child is presently in the father’s care pursuant to orders made on 12 September 2025.
These proceedings first came before me on 18 February 2026 for case management hearing, where I adjourned the matter to 12 March 2026 for interim hearing of the mother’s Application in a Proceeding, and where I granted the father’s lawyers leave to withdraw from the proceedings. In addition, I also made orders, inter alia, for the father to file a Response to Application in a Proceeding and any affidavit material upon which he sought to rely. The father appeared that day, after his lawyers withdrew, and participated in the hearing and acknowledged his understanding of the directions.
On 12 March 2026, the father again appeared self-represented but indicated that he had obtained a grant of legal aid and would soon obtain legal advice and representation. The father had not filed documents in accordance with the directions of 18 February 2026 and had not filed a Notice of Address for Service.
The father agreed that he had been served with the documents upon which the mother sought to rely. The father made an oral application for an adjournment such that he have an opportunity to obtain the assistance and advice of lawyers, and to enable him to address the mother’s Amended Application in a Proceeding (the amended version of which was filed two days prior to the interim hearing).
In circumstances where the mother had substantially expanded the relief she sought in her Amended Application in a Proceeding (including as to the father undertaking a psychiatric assessment), I identified that it would be procedurally unfair to the father should he not be granted an adjournment such that he could consider those matters prior to a contested hearing.
I advised the parties that I proposed to defer consideration of the amended part of the mother’s application until a later date (in addition to other less pressing matters), thereby allowing the father additional time to obtain legal advice and have the assistance of lawyers in the preparation of his court documents.
However, I indicated to the parties that I would hear submissions as to the injunctive relief sought by the mother about the father’s social media posting, as set out in her Application in a Proceeding, given the serious issues identified on the mother’s behalf in the affidavit of her solicitor. Those matters were well known to the father and he was on notice that those matters were to be dealt with by the court at the hearing.
The mother’s solicitor advocate identified the documents the mother read for the purposes of the hearing and a series of documents became exhibits. Those exhibits included ten recordings made by the father and uploaded to various social media accounts (including Tik Tok, Instagram and Facebook) (Exhibit 6). The father did not object to my receipt of those recordings and, in fact, was eager that I should review them. The father also accepted as accurate a transcript made by the mother’s solicitor of the social media video post he had made in March 2026 and he did not object to that transcript being admitted into evidence (Exhibit 7).
I also made the updated Family Report of the Court Child Expert, dated 10 March 2026, an exhibit (Exhibit 8).
I have reviewed the documents identified on behalf of the mother and the documents which became exhibits, including the video postings made by the father and the transcript.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) supported the making of injunctions against the father.

INJUNCTIONS

The mother’s application invites consideration of ss 68B, 114Q, and 114R of the Act.
The mother relevantly sought the following orders:
The father be restrained by injunction from discussing the family law proceedings with anyone other than his legal representatives or publishing any information pertaining to the family law proceedings.
Part XIVB of the Act (which contains ss 114Q and 114R) sets out the restrictions on the publication of information arising from proceedings under the Act.
Section 114N of the Act provides a simplified outline of Part XIVB, as follows:
It is an offence to communicate an account of proceedings under this Act to the public, if the account identifies certain people involved in the proceedings.

It is an offence to communicate a list of proceedings that are to be dealt with under this Act to the public, and that are identified by reference to the names of the parties to those proceedings.

A communication is not made to the public if the communication is made to a person with a significant and legitimate interest in the subject matter of the communication that is greater than the interest of members of the public generally.
To communicate means to communicate by any means including by means of the internet (s 114P(1)(d)) and it is an indictable offence to communicate to the public an account of proceedings that identifies parties or others involved in the proceedings (s 114Q) and to communicate to the public a list of court (s 114R).
For the purposes of s 114Q, an account of proceedings is taken to identify a person if the account includes material that is sufficient to identify the person to a member of the public including but not limited to, a picture, recording, or physical description of the person, or a name or title that identifies a person (s 114Q(3)).
The Full Court in Sitwell & Sitwell [2014] FamCAFC 5 (“Sitwell”) considered circumstances which might enliven the court making an injunction to prevent the publication of an account of proceedings as contemplated by the former s 121 of the Act, now s 114Q of the Act. The Full Court held:
Gibb & Gibb and Re Schwarzkopff comprise authority for the proposition that an injunction merely restating the effect of FLA s 121 is unnecessary and undesirable. Such an injunction should only be granted in special circumstances... Clearly, each case must be considered on its merits, but there can be no doubt that the mere fact that the proposed publication of material is likely to contravene FLA s 121 is insufficient to justify the granting of an injunction to prevent the publication of that material. In our opinion, evidence to the effect that the material to be published is likely to impact upon the welfare or best interests of children may be sufficient to demonstrate “special circumstances”. Similarly, evidence to the effect that the material to be published contains scandalous disparagement of courts or judicial officers which is likely to impair their authority, or serious and baseless attacks on the integrity or impartiality of courts or judicial officers, may also be sufficient to demonstrate “special circumstances”, particularly if it is apparent that the good sense of the community may not amount to a sufficient safeguard against such disparagement. It is unnecessary to provide further examples. The category of circumstances which might fairly be described as “special”, whether individually or in combination with other circumstances, is not closed.
(Emphasis added)
As summarised by Forrest J in Xuarez & Vitela [2012] FamCA 574 (at [35]-[36]), in relation to the former s 121, but equally applicable to the current s 114Q:
Clearly, proceedings for an offence against s 121 must be heard on indictment and tried by a jury and can only be commenced by or with the written consent of the Commonwealth Director of Public Prosecutions.
This Court does not itself prosecute or try persons who might be considered to have contravened s 121... At best, the Court can direct the Marshall of the Court and/or the Australian Federal Police to investigate and take steps to ensure any breach of s 121 of the Family Law Act is prosecuted. See Prentice & Bellas and Anor [2012] FamCA 108 per Murphy J.

CONSIDERATION

The parties have been involved in proceedings concerning their child for many years. In April 2021, final orders were made for the child to live with the mother and spend four nights per fortnight with the father.
In the subsequent years, there have been further court proceedings.
In mid-2025, the mother experienced a mental health crisis, which resulted in her involuntary hospitalisation for a month. The child was voluntarily placed in the father’s care by agreement between the mother, the father and the Department of Communities and Justice (“DCJ”) (Exhibit 8 at [7]). In subsequent interim proceedings, orders have been made for the child to remain living with the father and to spend unsupervised time with the mother. The mother sought the review of the orders made on 12 September 2025, which challenge was dismissed by orders made on 12 January 2026. I have had regard to the reasons for judgment of Deputy Chief Justice McClelland (Leppins & Leppins [2026] FedCFamC1F 2).
The mother determined to return to live in her country of origin in mid-December 2025, where she has family support. On a final basis, the mother seeks orders that the child live with her in that country.
The father seeks orders on a final basis that the child continue to live with him and spend time with the mother in this country.
When this matter was before the court on 12 March 2026, the court was made aware, on behalf of the mother, that certain events had occurred previously, which resulted in the father being taken for emergency review by a mental health team at a hospital. The father’s brief account of those matters, largely accorded with the summary given by the mother’s solicitor about those events. The father confirmed that previously he had been taken to hospital by the police and kept there, against his will, for a number of hours.
In response to my inquiry, the father advised that the child had been cared for by members of the father’s family that night, while these events had occurred. The father indicated that the child was not at home that morning but also was not attending school that day. The potential risks for the child arising from these events are clear and need to be addressed by evidence at the adjourned hearing.
The above summary makes clear that each of the parents have had, and may continue to experience, challenges to their mental health and functioning. The child is seven years old and is dependent upon the adults caring for him to act protectively of him and in his best interests.
My consideration of the identified material posted by the father occurs in the context of this complex matter where the mother’s mental health remains vulnerable and she identifies additional distress at the prospect of private matters between the parties and details of their child and these court events, being broadcast to any portion of the public. The mother also identifies a history of alleged family violence by the father to the mother during the relationship (Exhibit 8 at [15] and [16]) which is denied by the father.
On my review of the material posted by the father, it appears:
(a) He uses his own first and last name in his postings. He submitted to the court that he is a “digital creator” approved by some or all of the administrators of the various platforms he uses.

(b) He has identified the child by name, and he has identified the mother variously by occasional use of her name and also by reference to her as his wife, his ex-wife and as the mother of the child.

(c) In some posts, the father has uploaded images of himself, the child and the mother along with narrative about the circumstances when the image was taken and with commentary as to the current status of the ongoing court proceedings.

(d) He identifies that proceedings have occurred in this court and are continuing, and he provides, on occasion, his narrative of what he says has happened at particular court events.

(e) He makes comments about his view of the proceedings and its outcomes and is critical of the process, the court, and the lawyers involved.

(f) He identifies the mother’s former lawyer by name and has posted an image of a car which he declares is that of the mother’s former lawyer. That image includes the licence plate for the car.

(g) He has posted a mobile telephone number which he says belongs to the mother’s former lawyer.

(h) He has spoken of the mother’s mental health vulnerability, including particulars of medication which has been prescribed for the mother.

(i) He has spoken of the mother’s past attempts to end her life by suicide and the circumstances of her subsequent hospitalisation. The father discloses matters observed by him during visits to the hospital (and his account of the mother while hospitalised). He also gives an account of things he says were shared with him and comments made by staff caring for the mother at that time.

(j) He has posted parts of an email exchange between him, the mother’s current lawyer, and the ICL to a social media platform. The father has captioned his comments as “Introducing my ex-wife’s current lawyer who used to work for (named practitioner)”. He has then shared images which include the email sent to the lawyers, the court number for these proceedings and what appears to be an image of details for the child on the school’s register (which includes the mother’s former solicitor’s mobile contact details).

The mother asserts that in some of the father’s social media posts, he has attached audio recordings made by him of the mother, the making of which occurred without her consent and the publication of which has occurred without her knowledge or agreement. The mother also seeks to restrain the father from making such recordings in the future, which recording in any event would likely be a breach of the Surveillance Devices Act 2007 (NSW). In circumstances where the mother is living in another country and where communications between the parties may not be regularly occurring, I defer further consideration of those matters until the next occasion.
The Court Child Expert (on 17 February 2026) asked the father about the mother’s expressed concerns in relation to the social media postings by the father. The Court Child Expert reports as follows (in paragraph 17 of Exhibit 8):
When asked about his recent alleged social media postings, (the father) appeared to acknowledge that he has made another post on the (school) parents WhatsApp group, sharing a link to his other social media postings. (The father) was vague about the content of these postings and his conversation at this point took on a distinctly rambling, threatening, and grandiose tone. He seemed to be expressing the view that members of the legal profession, including lawyers acting for (the mother), had engaged in misconduct, stating that he was going public to “clear my name” and “when it does come out it’s going to shock a lot of people.
While I do not know if the father accepts the accuracy of the summary made by the Court Child Expert of this part of their discussions, the views attributed to the father in that paragraph appear largely consistent with my review of the father’s social media material exhibited before the court. The father repeatedly declared in his submissions that he wanted “the truth to come out” about all he had been through in the many years he had been involved in family law proceedings. The fact that the father presently has the child living with him did not seem to displace the father’s belief that he had been victimised throughout. The bases for the father’s complaints were not easily discerned either from his submissions or my review of the material to which I was referred.
There are some difficulties with the relief as sought on behalf of the mother, including the very wide ambit of one part of the restraint sought by her (which, if made, might have the effect of preventing the father even from talking to family, friends or medical and allied health providers, about his experiences in these proceedings).
The father has been advised by the mother’s lawyers that their client objects to the contents of his postings and to the sharing of private information about their family and these proceedings.
The father has been served with the mother’s Application in a Proceeding (since before 18 February 2026 when he had lawyers on the record assisting him) which makes clear her objection. I also urged caution upon the father when the matter was first before me on that date.
These circumstances distinguish this case from the circumstances which applied in Sitwell where the wife was seeking restraints be made in the face of threatened conduct by the husband only (that is, a threatened breach of a statutory prohibition). Here, the father has engaged in repeated publications, including in the face of objection on behalf of the mother.
These are parenting proceedings and power exists under s 68B of the Act, to grant such injunction as the court considers appropriate for the welfare of the child. The court also has power under s 114(3) of the Act to grant an injunction where it appears just or convenient to do so.
The father has repeatedly posted digital and written content to social media which may lead to the identification of parties who are involved in family law proceedings. I am not required to determine if his conduct does amount to a contravention of s 114Q of the Act. However, in considering the restraints sought on behalf of the mother, consideration of what has been undertaken by the father assists in assessment of the risk of the continuation of such behaviour by the father. Review of the material posted would readily permit a member of the public, especially those who may know the father, the mother, or the family more generally, to identify the child and the family as participants in family law proceedings. Some of the father’s posts have, it appears, been shared by him within the parent cohort of the school at which the child attends. In some of the posts, the father provides his narrative of parts of these proceedings.
The deeply personal information shared by the father in some of those postings creates the real prospect that the mother, whose mental health remains vulnerable, will experience embarrassment and distress consequently. The private information of the young child is disclosed casually by the father in his postings, with little apparent regard for how the child might feel about the father’s accounts of personal matters in the life of the child. Should the child become aware of the postings, they may cause the child embarrassment and distress. The child is entitled to respect and s 114Q is in part designed to ensure protections to persons and children engaged in proceedings before this Court. Among other things, the actions by his father disregard the child’s right to privacy.
In addition, I note that parts of the postings made by the father include disclosure of particulars of lawyers who have previously acted, or who are continuing to act, on behalf of the mother. Some of the father’s postings include comments which are disparaging of (identified) legal practitioners and of the court and he makes baseless allegations of “federal corruption” impacting these family law proceedings and some, possibly, threatening content (suggesting the recipients ought “to pack their toothbrushes”, a comment possibly intending to suggest a risk of future imprisonment).
The father has published privileged legal communications between himself, the mother’s lawyer and the ICL, despite objection by the mother’s lawyers about this.
I am satisfied that the material published by the father on the various social media platforms creates exceptional circumstances because of its potential adverse effects on the welfare of the child and on other persons involved in the child’s care (notably the mother). That material remains available at large and can be shared and, or, re-posted by viewers, such that the extent of dissemination is at large.
I will make orders which require the father to immediately remove the various postings he has made which may identify the participants in these proceedings and I will also restrain the father from making such postings into the future. I am persuaded to grant an ongoing restraint against the father as he has been on notice of the objection to his actions for an extended period of time and has also disregarded the cautions I expressed to him when the matter was before me on a prior occasion.
The proposed wording of the future restraint, as sought on behalf of the mother, was too wide. The father must be permitted to speak to his friends and family and medical or allied health providers, about his circumstances and matters impacting him and which may include these family law proceedings. Recognition of the support parties receive from family and friends and their own (personal) online community informed, in part, the recent amendments of the former s 121 of the Act. The new s 114S makes clear that publication to family is an exception to the non-publication requirements.
However, I accept that the father has gone well beyond seeking the support of friends and family via social media and is publishing content at large, including on Tik Tok, Instagram and to the child’s school community. I am not aware of the extent of the father’s publications on Facebook (whether his postings are public or to a closed group only) but in any event, the matters identified above satisfy me that the publications undertaken by the father warrant restraint.
The balance of the mother’s Amended Application in a Proceeding will be considered on the next occasion.
